revision.text: {{Game |Guid=f517d120-86f5-4b09-8681-12263b203c0e |Name=Deltarune Chapter 5 |Image=Ch5-trailer-YzI66DkClTeJ5jBQ.png |Deck=The Deltarune saga continues. |ReleaseDateType=Full |ReleaseDate=2026-06-24 |Concepts=Concepts/Digital Distribution, Concepts/Sequel |Developers=Companies/tobyfox, Companies/8 4 Ltd |Franchises=Franchises/Undertale |Genres=Genres/Role Playing |Platforms=Platforms/Mac, Platforms/PC, Platforms/Nintendo Switch, Platforms/Nintendo Switch 2, Platforms/PlayStation 4, Platforms/PlayStation 5 |Publishers=Companies/tobyfox }} == Overview == Released on June 24, 2026 Deltarune Chapter 5 is the release of the fifth chapter in the episodic follow-up to [[Games/Undertale|Undertale]].

page.title: Games/Demon Slayer Kimetsu no Yaiba The Hinokami Chronicles 2/Credits

revision.text:
{{Game | Name=Densha de Go! | Guid=3030-36690 | Deck=The first in a series of arcade-style electric train simulators by Taito, featuring a unique arcade control panel that gives the illusion of driving actual trains through four real-life Japanese train routes. | ReleaseDate=1997-03-01 | ReleaseDateType=Month | Characters=Characters/Tetsu_chan | Concepts=Concepts/Train_Driving_Simulation | Developers=Companies/CyberFront_Corporation,Companies/Taito_Corporation,Companies/Taito_GM_R_And_D_Sect_2,Companies/Unbalance_Corporation | Franchises=Franchises/Densha_de_Go,Franchises/Simple,Franchises/Simple_1500 | Genres=Genres/Driving_Racing,Genres/Simulation | Locations=Locations/Japan | Objects=Objects/Train | Platforms=Platforms/Arcade,Platforms/Game_Boy_Color,Platforms/PC,Platforms/PlayStation,Platforms/Saturn,Platforms/WonderSwan | Publishers=Companies/CyberFront_Corporation,Companies/D3_Publisher_Inc,Companies/Taito_Corporation,Companies/Unbalance_Corporation | Games=Games/Densha_Daisuki_Plarail_ga_Ippai,Games/Train_Simulator_2014 | Themes= | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/2/23093/2704350-denshadego_ps1.jpg }}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2704350-denshadego_ps1.jpg","path":"2\/23093\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_small,square_mini,square_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,screen_kubrick","caption":"PlayStation JP box art"},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== Densha de Go! (loosely translated to "Go by Train!") is an [[Objects/Train|electric train]] driving simulation game developed and released by [[Companies/Taito_Corporation|Taito]] for [[Platforms/Arcade|arcades]] in Japan on March 1997.
The first in [[Franchises/Densha_de_Go|a series of arcade train driving simulators]], Densha de Go! tasks players with driving an electric passenger train through parts of four real-life [[Locations/Japan|Japanese]] train routes: the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San%27in_Main_Line San'in Main Line] in [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture Kyoto Prefecture], the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keihin%E2%80%93T%C5%8Dhoku_Line Keihin-Tōhoku Line] between [[Locations/Tokyo|Tokyo]] and [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kanagawa_Prefecture Kanagawa] Prefectures, the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line Tōkaidō Main Line] between Kyoto and [[Locations/Osaka|Osaka]] Prefectures, and the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yamanote_Line Yamanote Line] in Tokyo Prefecture.
As train drivers, players must adhere to a strict timetable while obeying the speed limit and arriving to each station as cleanly and close to the "stopping point" as possible. The game uses a time-based penalty system, where players have 30 seconds of penalty time that depletes when certain penalties occur (such as failing to observe the speed limit or arriving in an unsatisfactory way) and [[Concepts/Game_Over|ends the credit prematurely]] when it reaches zero.
The game features a unique control panel stylized after a train driver's cab (more specifically the cab of a [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/205_series 205-series train]), featuring a five-stage throttle lever, a nine-stage brake lever, a horn button, and several decorative gauges (with a "working" analog speedometer and signal light). It also features a seat that vibrates when the train crosses certain areas. Two versions of the cabinet were released: a compact one housing a 29-inch screen and a deluxe one housing a 50-inch screen.
The game later received an update on September 1997, known as ''Densha de Go! EX''. While it mainly focuses on bug-fixes, it adds some new hidden EXTRA versions of some routes (adding [[Concepts/Rain|rainy weather]]). The original version was later included in an update to the 2017 arcade game [https://www.giantbomb.com/densha-de-go/3030-81611 Densha de Go!!] (as a bonus game mode, known as "Fukkoku-ban").
==Routes== The game includes four main routes, each using a particular type of train. Two of these routes (San'in Main Line and Tōkaidō Main Line) have hidden "EXTRA" versions, while the remaining two routes (Keihin-Tōhoku Line and Yamanote Line) only have hidden EXTRA versions in the game's EX update. The PS1 and GBC versions omit all EXTRA versions.
* [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San%27in_Main_Line '''San'in Main Line'''] ([https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%9B%BD%E9%89%84%E3%82%AD%E3%83%8F58%E7%B3%BB%E6%B0%97%E5%8B%95%E8%BB%8A KiHa 58-series]) - Runs eastward from Kameoka Station in [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kameoka,_Kyoto Kameoka] to Kyoto Station in [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shimogyo-ku,_Kyoto Shimogyō-ku]. The original version of the route is the game's only beginner route and has no bonus mini-game. While the EXTRA version includes the entire route, the original version is very short and omits the segments between Saga-Arashiyama Station and Tambaguchi Station. * '''[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keihin%E2%80%93T%C5%8Dhoku_Line Keihin–Tōhoku Line]''' ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/209_series 209-series]) - Runs southward from Shinagawa Station in [[Locations/Tokyo|Tokyo]] to Yokohama Station in [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yokohama Yokohama]. The EXTRA version is the same, but with rainy weather. * [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line '''Tōkaidō Main Line'''] ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/221_series 221-series]) - Runs southward from Kyoto Station in Shimogyō-ku to Ōsaka Station in [[Locations/Osaka|Osaka]]. Unlike other routes, this one is a [https://ja.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%BF%AB%E9%80%9F%E5%88%97%E8%BB%8A "rapid service"] that skips multiple stations along the way, allowing for much faster speeds. The EXTRA version starts farther ahead, at Takatsuki Station, but stops at additional stations along the way. * [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yamanote_Line '''Yamanote Line'''] ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/205_series 205-series]) - Runs clockwise from Shibuya Station to Tokyo Station. The EXTRA version is the same, but with rainy weather.
==Ports & Re-Releases== The game received two console ports, each with special controller peripherals made for the game:
* The original version was ported to the [[Platforms/PlayStation|Sony PlayStation]] on December 18, 1997. While it does not include the EXTRA route versions, it adds an introductory mode that follows only one segment in the San'in Main Line (between Kameoka Station and Umahori Station) with no restrictions. This version later received a re-release as part of [[Companies/D3_Publisher_Inc|D3Publisher's]] [[Franchises/Simple|Simple 1500]] series of budget games, as ''Simple 1500 Series Vol. 103: The Gansho Densha Utenshi - Densha de Go!'', on January 30, 2003. * The EX version was ported to the [[Platforms/Saturn|Sega Saturn]] on October 1, 1998. This version features two unlockable modes: Free Mode (which starts the player with 999 seconds) and Snow Mode (which uses snowy weather). The original version of the game was also ported to [https://www.giantbomb.com/pc/3045-94 Windows PCs] (by [https://www.giantbomb.com/unbalance-corporation/3010-3427 Unbalance] on April 23, 1999) and received two handheld adaptations: one for the [https://www.giantbomb.com/wonderswan/3045-65 WonderSwan] (on March 4, 1999), and one for the [https://www.giantbomb.com/game-boy-color/3045-57 Game Boy Color] (by [https://www.giantbomb.com/cyberfront-corporation/3010-579 CyberFront] on December 10, 1999).

{{Game |Guid=2baa3a0d-ace9-486e-80f5-f6d14e9b12e8 |Name=Design and Conjure |Image=https://giantbomb.com/api/media/file/media--file--design-and-conjure-06-15-2026.jpg |BackgroundImage=https://giantbomb.com/api/media/file/media--file--design-and-conjureb-06-15-2026.webp |Deck=Solve puzzles and decorate this witch's hometown. |ReleaseDateType=None |Concepts=Concepts/Indie, Concepts/Digital Distribution, Concepts/Steam, Concepts/Witch, Concepts/Female Protagonists, Concepts/Cleaning, Concepts/Polygonal 3D |Developers=Companies/Tiny Kiwi Games |Genres=Genres/Puzzle, Genres/Simulation |Platforms=Platforms/PC |Publishers=Companies/Tiny Kiwi Games }}

revision.text: {{Game |Guid=231c5921-997e-4500-a8a9-51699cc2015d |Name=Doraemon Comic Traveler |Aliases=Doraemon Browser Games |Image=Doraemoncover.png |Caption=Doraemon Comic Traveler is a free-to-play online role-playing browser game published by CTW, Inc. on the G123 platform. |ReleaseDateType=Full |ReleaseDate=2025-12-15 |Characters=Characters/Doraemon |Concepts=Concepts/RPG Elements, Concepts/Roguelike |Developers=Companies/CTW |Franchises=Franchises/Doraemon |Genres=Genres/Adventure, Genres/Strategy, Genres/MMORPG |Platforms=Platforms/Browser |Publishers=Companies/CTW |Games=Games/Chrome, Games/Firefox |Themes=Themes/Anime }} Doraemon Comic Traveler is a free-to-play online role-playing browser game published by CTW, Inc. on the G123 platform. Based on the Doraemon manga franchise by Fujiko F. Fujio and the Doraemon's Long Tales feature-length film series, the title is the first online RPG adaptation of the franchise and combines branching text-adventure stage progression with rogue-like combat. Players join Doraemon, Nobita, Shizuka, Gian, and Suneo as they travel to an original setting called COMIC PLANET and cooperate with native COMIC ALIENS through stages drawn from the world of the Long Tales series.

{{Game |Guid=5b320138-db4b-4d0f-974a-ba9274ad42cb |Name=Dragon Ball XenoVerse 3 |Aliases=Dragon Ball Project Age 1000 |Deck=The third Dragon Ball XenoVerse title takes place in the Age of 1000, several hundred years after the last Dragon Ball Z/Super story. |ReleaseDateType=Year |YearPart=2027 |Characters=Characters/Goku, Characters/Bulma, Characters/Piccolo, Characters/Gamma 1, Characters/Krillin, Characters/Yamcha, Characters/Trunks, Characters/Vegeta, Characters/Great Ape, Characters/Broly, Characters/Appule |Concepts=Concepts/Character Creation, Concepts/Saiyan, Concepts/Namekian, Concepts/Super Saiyan, Concepts/Licensed Game, Concepts/Games Based on Anime, Concepts/Flight, Concepts/Flyting Game, Concepts/Steam, Concepts/Digital Distribution |Developers=Companies/Dimps Corporation |Franchises=Franchises/Dragon Ball, Franchises/Dragon Ball Xenoverse |Genres=Genres/Fighting |Objects=Objects/Scouter, Objects/Cape, Objects/Turban, Objects/Armor |Platforms=Platforms/Xbox Series X S, Platforms/PlayStation 5, Platforms/PC |Publishers=Companies/Bandai Namco Entertainment |Themes=Themes/Anime }}
